nativeRange.habitat: Shallow warm-water neritic zones to approximately 2 m depth. Inhabits estuary and lagoon environments, mangrove margins, seagrass beds, sandy backreef flats, and calm reef-adjacent areas. Anchors into soft sandy substrate via buried rhizoidal holdfast; commonly found alongside Udotea and Halimeda in Caribbean sand-substrate communities.

decorPreferences: deep sandy substrate (minimum 5 cm) for rhizoidal holdfast burial
open display area with strong overhead lighting
low-flow or calm zone to prevent uprooting
stable placement away from heavy herbivore grazing areas

avoidWith: urchins that may dislodge or consume the holdfast
large herbivorous fish such as tangs that may graze calcareous algae
strong current or powerhead flow directed at the specimen

imageCandidates.notes: John Hepburn; 1024x768. Sharp, well-lit in-situ shot of a single complete thallus showing the diagnostic calcified stalk, buried holdfast, and dark-green brush-like capitulum. Best representative of the species morphology.
Eric Knight; 1024x768. Whole plant standing in sandy seagrass habitat, showing stalk and brush cap in natural setting; slightly hazy water so kept as non-recommended context shot.
nebrooks; 576x1024. Close-up of the brush-like capitulum held in hand, showing the loosely arranged filament texture; stalk not visible so kept as non-recommended detail shot.

summary: The Pencil Top (Penicillus capitatus) is a calcareous green macroalga with a stiff calcified stalk topped by a brush-like cap, anchored in sand by buried rhizoids. Native to the Caribbean and tropical Atlantic, it is notably short-lived in captivity — specimens typically survive weeks to a few months — and requires high lighting, stable calcium and magnesium, and a sand bed at least 5 cm deep.
careNotes:
The Pencil Top is a calcareous green macroalga in the family Udoteaceae — the same family as the closely related Mermaid's Tail (Udotea sp.) and the same genus as Merman's Shaving Brush ([sw-algae-060]). Each plant (known as a thallus) consists of a stiff, heavily calcified cylindrical stalk topped by a broad brush-like cap called a capitulum, which spreads roughly 5–8 cm across and is made up of loosely arranged hollow filaments. Mature specimens reach approximately 5–15 cm in total height. The base anchors into soft sandy substrate through a buried tangle of root-like strands called rhizoids. In the wild, the Pencil Top grows in shallow warm-water lagoons, seagrass beds, estuaries, and sandy areas bordering reefs throughout the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, Mediterranean, and eastern Australia, typically in water no deeper than about 2 m.

SHORT LIFESPAN — CRITICAL CARE NOTE: Penicillus capitatus is well-documented as semelparous — meaning each individual plant reproduces once and then dies — with individual thalli reported to live as little as 45 days to approximately 8 weeks, and some specimens living nearly twice as long. In captivity, this inherently short lifespan is widely reported as a significant challenge; specimens frequently decline and die within weeks even in well-maintained reef systems. Specimen death within weeks to a couple of months is generally expected and should not be interpreted as a husbandry failure; however, optimal conditions may extend the window somewhat. Purchasing multiple specimens and attempting fragmentation propagation is generally advised to maintain a continuous display presence.

SIBLING SPECIES: P. capitatus and P. dumetosus (Merman's Shaving Brush, [sw-algae-060]) belong to the same genus and share essentially identical care requirements, the same semelparous life history, and the same sand-anchoring morphology. The principal distinction visible to hobbyists is minor variation in capitulum density; for aquarium purposes both species can be treated identically.

SAND BED REQUIREMENT: A sand bed of at least 5 cm is considered necessary to allow the rhizoidal holdfast to anchor properly. Without adequate sand depth, the thallus tends to tip over and fail to establish.

CALCIUM AND ALKALINITY: As a calcareous alga that deposits calcium carbonate (aragonite) into its stalk and capitulum, P. capitatus benefits from stable reef-grade calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm), and alkalinity (8–12 dKH). Drops in calcium or magnesium have been reported to accelerate thallus decline.

LIGHTING: High-intensity lighting is generally advised. Calcareous green algae in the Udoteaceae are consistently documented as requiring ample light to support photosynthesis and active calcification. Positioning under strong overhead lighting is commonly recommended.

FLOW: Low to moderate flow is generally preferred — sufficient for gas exchange but not strong enough to dislodge or disturb the sandy substrate anchor.

PALATABILITY: The heavily calcified structure is considered largely unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates, giving Pencil Top reasonable grazing resistance in a community reef. Urchins and some large herbivorous fish such as tangs may still damage or dislodge specimens.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is generally attempted by fragmentation — detaching a healthy section of the brush or stalk base and anchoring it into sand — though success rates are variable given the short natural lifespan. Sexual reproduction via gamete dispersal is not a practical captive propagation method. Because Penicillus is semelparous (each thallus reproduces once and then dies), maintaining the species in a display tank long-term typically requires purchasing new specimens or successfully propagating fragments before the parent thallus declines.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source found for this specialty entry. Product listing confirms scientific name Penicillus capitatus, common name 'Pencil Top', heavily calcified sand-substrate macroalga with brush-like top, low palatability to most marine fish and invertebrates. Minimal care detail on product page.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Penicillus capitatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: family Udoteaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae, division Chlorophyta. Distribution: Gulf of Mexico, Caribbean, Eastern South America, Mediterranean, eastern Australia. Habitat: shallow neritic zones to ~2 m, lagoon/estuary/seagrass/mangrove environments. Morphology: stipe + capitulum (5–8 cm diameter), mature height ~15 cm. Lifespan: ~45 days to ~8 weeks per thallus (semelparous), some specimens reported living nearly twice as long. High-stakes fields cross-checked against congeneric P. dumetosus data in sibling file sw-algae-060.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
